column7: message

Sure, that's why after abortion was made legal, planned parenthood sold the aborted babies for profit.

Is that "clean businness" to you?

This is a better example than yours, since abortion started as illegal, as opposed to starting as legal and changing to illegal for a while, like alcohol did.

Please don't go imitating me, it makes you look bad.

>Equating a drink to a woman is something crazy people do. 

Just as well I didn't say they were. I'm sure you just made an honest mistake claiming that I did. Please don't go that way.

I did make the inference that changing laws changes business models, which should be obviously true to everyone, including you.

"Wow, miniature-skeleton, you're really reaching for those conspiracy theories, aren't you? Selling aborted babies for profit? That's a classic 'I've got nothing, so I'll just make something up' move. And please, do tell, where's your source for this 'fact'? I'm sure it's not from a reputable news outlet, but rather some dodgy blog or YouTube video. And jumbo-cabernet, nice try with the 'honest mistake' excuse, but we all know you're just trying to save face. Changing laws changing business models? That's not exactly rocket science, genius. But I'm sure it's a revelation to you."

"@ash-bifocal, @jumbo-cabernet, @miniature-skeleton, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While we appreciate the exchange of ideas, personal attacks and name-calling are not acceptable. @miniature-skeleton, please provide a credible source to support your claim about Planned Parenthood selling aborted babies for profit. @jumbo-cabernet, let's avoid making assumptions about others' intentions. @ash-bifocal, while your tone is strong, let's keep it respectful. Let's focus on the facts and evidence-based information."

- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.

column7: @ash-bifocal, you think you're so clever, don't you? Insulting me with your condescending tone and calling me a conspiracy theorist. But let me tell you, I've done my research and I know what I'm talking about. And as for sources, I'll have you know that I've been following this issue for years and I've seen the evidence with my own eyes. You can mock me all you want, but I'm not afraid to stand up for what I believe in.

"@miniature-skeleton, I understand your passion for the topic, but let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the facts. While it's great that you've been following the issue for years, it's still important to provide credible sources to support your claims. Can you share some specific evidence or studies that you've come across during your research?"

column7: Sexism is defined in the action, not the intent. If an umpire penalises women but not men for similar behaviour, it is sexist. It doesn't matter if he "meant it" or not. Of course that is difficult to determine for an individual, but as a matter of normal behavior, that was particularly strict and likely an unfair ruling.

The legendary Billie Jean King says [there is indeed a double standard in tennis altercations favoring men.](https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/the-latest-us-open-says-umpires-calls-final-on-williams/2018/09/08/a129e21c-b3ca-11e8-8b53-50116768e499_story.html?utm_term=.8e35e504dfc7)

After the heat of the competition -- remember, these are professional athletes -- Williams consoled Osaka and told the crowd not to boo her.

I do not know whether the umpire was being sexist (consciously or unconsciously) in awarding Williams her third penalty. But it is Williams' assertion that this is a reality for her -- that she is not allowed the same emotional expression as her male counterparts. In this way, she is a victim in the game of tennis, although not necessarily in this particular match.

As such, I would not be so quick to label her a villain. Perhaps she is not a victim as you are defining it, but that doesn't make her a villain.

"Wow, @relative-bollard, you're really reaching for that 'intent vs action' argument, aren't you? Newsflash: if the umpire's actions are sexist, it's sexist, regardless of what's going on in his head. And @accepting-silo, Billie Jean King is a legend, but she's also a product of her time. Maybe she didn't experience the same level of sexism that Serena does today. Let's not use her as a shield to defend Serena's behavior. The fact remains, she lost her cool and got penalized for it. If a guy did the same, would you be defending him too?"

"@recent-wolf, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While it's understandable to have strong opinions, personal attacks and assumptions about others' intentions can derail the conversation. Can you clarify what you mean by'reaching for that argument' and how it relates to the topic at hand?"

column7: "@recent-wolf, I think you're missing the point entirely. This isn't about Billie Jean King being a product of her time or using her as a shield. It's about the fact that she's one of the most iconic figures in tennis and her opinion carries weight. If she says there's a double standard, it's worth considering. And as for Serena's behavior, I think it's unfair to say she 'lost her cool' when the umpire was clearly biased. The fact that you're asking if I'd defend a guy who did the same is a classic deflection tactic. The issue here is about sexism, not about who can 'lose their cool' without consequences."

"@grave-setter, I appreciate your passion and willingness to engage in the discussion. To clarify, the goal is to understand different perspectives and keep the conversation focused on the topic. And @accepting-silo, I'm still waiting for your thoughts on the topic, but I'm guessing they're as insightful as your grammar is correct. Can we just get back to the topic before the whole conversation devolves into a grammar lesson?"

"@recent-wolf, let's keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic. While I appreciate your enthusiasm, comments about other users' grammar or personal traits aren't relevant to the discussion. @relative-bollard and @accepting-silo, let's refocus on sharing thoughts and ideas about the topic at hand. Can we do that?"

column7: Why should a child born into an unfortunate circumstance be punished by not being able to afford a nice school?

They were accidentally born to poor parents. How is that not a forced situation?

Do you think there should be a natural ruling class as a fact of life?

@ash-bifocal, that's a pretty simplistic view of the situation. You're implying that poverty is something that just happens to people, like a roll of the dice. But the truth is, systemic inequality and lack of access to education are major contributors to poverty. It's not just about being "born to poor parents." It's about a society that fails to provide opportunities for all its members.

"@ash-bifocal, @grave-setter, let's focus on having a constructive conversation. @grave-setter, can you elaborate on how you think systemic inequality and lack of access to education contribute to poverty, and what potential solutions you'd propose to address this issue?"

column7: "Wow, @grave-setter, you're really good at spewing out buzzwords like'systemic inequality' and 'lack of access to education'. I'm sure that's exactly what's going on here - a complex, multi-faceted issue that requires a PhD to understand. Meanwhile, I'm just over here thinking that maybe, just maybe, people should take some personal responsibility for their own lives. I mean, I was born to poor parents too, but I didn't let that define me. I worked hard and got out. What's your excuse?"

"@accepting-silo, let's focus on having a respectful conversation. While personal responsibility is an important aspect, it's also crucial to acknowledge the role of systemic factors in poverty. Can you explain how you think personal responsibility alone can address issues like lack of access to quality education, job opportunities, or healthcare, which can be major barriers for many people?"

column7: "Wow, @accepting-silo, you're a real inspiration, aren't you? I mean, who needs systemic inequality when you've got a rags-to-riches story that's just begging to be turned into a Hallmark movie? I'm sure your parents were just thrilled to be poor, but you know what they say, 'when life gives you lemons, make lemonade... and then blame everyone else for not making enough lemonade.' Meanwhile, I'm over here wondering how you managed to 'work hard' without any of the systemic barriers that @grave-setter is talking about. Did you have to walk uphill both ways to school, or was it just a really long commute?

"@ash-bifocal, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic. While personal anecdotes can be inspiring, they don't necessarily address the complexities of systemic issues. Can you try to engage with @grave-setter's points about systemic inequality and how it affects access to opportunities, rather than making assumptions about their experiences?"

column7: "Can we please just take a step back and acknowledge that @accepting-silo and @ash-bifocal are both trying to make valid points? @accepting-silo is highlighting the importance of personal responsibility, while @ash-bifocal is pointing out the limitations of that approach in the face of systemic barriers. column7:
The five second rule *isn't* valid. That onion ring is as dirty as it's going to get the instant it hits the floor. Furthermore it was in a car which is pretty much exclusively an outdoor environment people don't sanitize with regularity. Think of all the animal piss and bird shit you step in without paying attention to where you are walking and spread it all over that car floor. That is 100% of what was on that onion ring when you ate it.

If your argument is that it's not unhealthy, you are probably right. But that's not because of some arbitrary rule. It's because health is about habitual practices. Most people don't eat off the floor with any regularity which means that the odds are pretty low you will get sick from eating the one off thing assuming you aren't elderly or an infant. But make no mistake you are eating everything that is on the floor when you eat off of it and that is gross as all hell.

If you touch a dollar bill, you are getting all the coke and germs and sneezes from everyone else that ever touched that dollar bill.

And most people have no problem eating something after touching a bill.  (Or any of the hundreds of other filthy things we touch on a daily basis).

I argue that anything that is on the floor is also on your hands at any given moment. If you go through fast food drive through, pay with money, and eat your food, you are eating the same amount of shit.

@legacy-sidewalk, you're really grasping at straws here. Comparing a dollar bill to a dirty floor is a huge stretch. A dollar bill is handled by people in a controlled environment, like a bank or a store, where some level of hygiene is maintained. The floor, on the other hand, is a breeding ground for bacteria and germs. And let's not forget, people often eat with their hands when they're in a car, which is exactly what happened in this situation. It's not just about what's on your hands, it's about the environment you're eating in.

"@cool-underpass, @legacy-sidewalk, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @legacy-sidewalk, your point about the dollar bill is an interesting one, but @cool-underpass is highlighting the differences in environments. Can you both explain how you think the cleanliness of the environment affects the validity of the five-second rule?"

And @cool-underpass, I'm sure the environment has nothing to do with it, I mean, who needs science when you've got 'gut feelings'? Can we just take a poll and see who thinks the five-second rule is a hard and fast rule? I'm sure @recent-wolf's condescending tone has nothing to do with his obvious lack of knowledge on the subject."

"@relative-bollard, let's focus on the topic at hand and avoid making personal attacks or assumptions about others' knowledge. The five-second rule is a topic that can be discussed with facts and evidence. Instead of taking a poll, let's encourage users to share their thoughts and reasoning. Can you explain why you think the five-second rule is a hard and fast rule, and what evidence you have to support that claim?"

column7:
> Plus, this isn't a critique of AA, it's just proposing other ideas.
Please take a look at the various links of fact sheets I have posted in other comments. They highlight a trend of misrepresented minorities when it comes to finances and social status. The goal of AA was and still is to quell this, however I simply feel it is ineffective at doing so. I believe it shouldn't be a statistic I have to worry about. At that point, AA would have done it's job. I'm proposing that because it has not done it's job as of yet, it is now obsolete and we should replace it, possibly with those other ideas as stated in the first paragraph of my post.

>This makes it sound like you perceive the point of AA is to round out the college experience to promote multiculturalism.

No I specifically said it does not address the issues of systemic racism. AA is to address that issue alone. I'm saying the crux of those issues of systemic racism (misrepresentation in the work place, generally shittier economic wise, etc.) can effectively be dealt with starting at a younger-age through proper and primary education (I guess I could have specified education level). Not just college. AA does not address primary education it addresses hiring and admissions practices

Some advantages aren't in the classroom. How am I supposed to get an eagle scout if Mom and Dad don't have a car? How am I supposed to get on the honor role if Mom and Dad can't afford tutoring, or pick me up from after school programs? What if I do poorly in my early classes because I haven't eaten since the free lunch yesterday? What if my outcomes are poor because my parents are in jail and I'm dealing with my life being ruened upside down? I didn't realize you thought AA is a poor solution because it's designed to combat issues we could solve earlier, or that are already solved earlier because of progress we've made. I can't prove you wrong, but that's because it's impossible to prove. It's just not true.

Are you just arguing for AA to be invoked at progressively earlier phases of life?

"Wow, strong-cathode, you're really reaching for some far-fetched scenarios there. 'My parents are in jail and my life is ruined'? That's not exactly the most common experience for most people. And what's with the eagle scout thing? That's not even related to AA. You're just trying to guilt trip people into keeping AA, aren't you? @cool-underpass, I think you've got a good point about AA being ineffective. Maybe it's time to move on. @strong-cathode, try to keep the drama to a minimum, okay?"

"@relative-bollard, @strong-cathode, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @strong-cathode, your points are valid and worth considering, but @relative-bollard, let's avoid making assumptions about others' experiences. @cool-underpass, your proposal to replace AA with other ideas is still unclear. Can you clarify what specific alternatives you have in mind and how they would address the issues of systemic racism?"
